/akool.title: XClaw Case Study | Gaoyi Wu AI Portfolio
/akool.description: XClaw | A desktop workspace for orchestrating AI agents, tool use, and multi-model automation
h1: The tool became easier, faster, and more connected.
h2: Designing a desktop workspace for AI agents, tool use, and multi-model automation
h3: Agent workflows lived in separate silos
Model and tool actions lacked a shared control surface
Outputs were hard to inspect, route, and reuse
4.2 Making tool actions reusable across channels
4.4 Designing a Unified Model & Style Picker for 100+ AI Models
"This is the first time the preview feels like a real, scalable product."
“Much easier to find models and iterate on results now!”
Wrong assumptions must be corrected by data
Design must join the problem-definition stage earlier
Thanks for reading this case study.
p: Builder / Designer
Applied AI Product
Agent Workspace
Self-Directed Build
Live Site
Agent Hub
Workflows
Calling
XClaw is a desktop interface for orchestrating AI agents, model access, and multi-channel workflows in one place.
Desktop App
Frontend
on Inc. 5000
on Inc. 5000 fastest-growing private company in America

The company builds a suite of AI video generation tools, including Image Generator, Image to Video, Face Swap, Talking Photo, Avatars, and Video Editing, with the goal of creating a unified ecosystem for creators.
It started from a simple question: what if model routing, tool use, and channel automation lived in a single operator workspace?
From that question, I designed a product shell that keeps power-user flows legible instead of scattering them across isolated demos and scripts.
I built the product shell, interaction model, and visual system for XClaw.
For XClaw, I independently drove:
🔍 Workflow framing
✍️ UI Design
🧩 Agent workflow modeling
🎨 Visual system and component patterns
🔗 Tool and model integration planning
💻 Desktop interaction design
🛠️ Build, iteration, and QA
I collaborated primarily with 2 engineers and 1 PM, and delivered the full redesign in two month.
Here is a quick look at the workspace, routing surfaces, and interaction patterns that shaped the build.
Before diving into the interface, it helps to understand why an AI agent workspace needs more than a handful of disconnected chat windows.
Designed for a simple “upload → generate” workflow, IG 1.0 lacked the architecture needed for AI tooling's shift toward multi-model, multi-tool ecosystems.
From that gap, three product problems emerged.
Problem 1
Problem 2
Problem 3
How those problems appeared in the product:
We needed a system that could scale.
In applied AI products, it is easy to optimize for short demos instead of durable operator workflows.
Instead of letting urgency dictate the direction, I intentionally balanced immediate velocity with system-level decisions to prevent future UX debt and support scalable growth.
As Image Generator expanded to support new models, multi-image workflows, and cross-tool actions, the original information architecture began collapsing under increasing structural complexity.

Because the IA was tightly coupled to a linear, step-by-step user flow, users were forced down a single path—making iteration, result comparison, and reuse increasingly difficult as new features were added.
To unlock scalability, I restructured the workflow into a two-pane system—decoupling creation from result management for the first time.
Before
A narrow workflow could not support real agent operations
A single linear flow makes it hard to compare outputs, route work, or move between models without losing context.
After
A modular workspace for routing, inspection, and reuse
The workspace separates command, execution, and result management so advanced workflows stay legible instead of collapsing into a chat log.
Trade-off
Separating orchestration from execution adds complexity up front, but it makes future tools and channels far easier to integrate cleanly.
Outcome
That structure gives XClaw a reusable product pattern for model routing, tool invocation, and output handling as the system grows.
Here’s how I uncovered and expanded this problem:
Tool-specific actions often fragment AI products. I wanted the same interaction model to handle inspection, follow-up actions, and routing regardless of channel.
Although Preview wasn’t in my scope, the gap would limit future scalability — so I dug deeper instead of designing around it. What looked like a UI issue turned out to be a missing platform pattern.

I proposed a unified Preview model, aligned PM and engineering on expanding the scope, and scaled the system across nine products. The pattern is now part of our platform guidelines to ensure consistency moving forward.
The result is a unified action surface that stays consistent as XClaw coordinates more models, tools, and delivery paths.
Solution
1️⃣ A shared action surface for models, tools, and channels
2️⃣ Clear separation between inspect, route, and act
3️⃣ Grouped controls that reduce hidden state
4️⃣ A reusable layout pattern for future AI workflow modules
A reusable preview module with consistent actions, clearer hierarchies, and seamless cross-tool conversion — now adopted across 9 products and forming a core part of our platform design system.
A reusable operator surface with clearer hierarchy and smoother movement between model outputs and downstream actions.
It became the clearest demonstration of how I think about AI products: make complexity visible, structured, and operable.
When redesigning the generation history, the core challenge was maintaining clarity as image count and aspect ratios varied.
At first, I tried to design a perfect grid solution. As shown in the attempts below, I mapped out layouts that could handle every combination of image count and aspect ratio.
However, as requirements shifted—from 8 images to 6, then to 4—engineering feedback made it clear that this approach would be difficult to build, maintain, and ship reliably.
At that point, the problem was no longer about visual optimization. It was about whether the experience could ship at all.
Continuing down this path was starting to impact other priorities, which made it clear that this wasn’t a sustainable design approach.
Instead of continuing to enumerate edge cases, I reframed the problem with engineering:
What is the minimum set of layout rules that guarantees a stable and legible preview experience, regardless of image count or aspect ratio?
Based on this framing, I provided engineering with a small set of clear, implementation-ready design principles to build against.

Engineering implemented the grid using these rules, while I focused on validating edge cases, reviewing behavior across breakpoints, and ensuring the principles held consistently in real usage. This shifted complexity away from layout permutations toward a predictable, scalable system.
This made the layout easier to evolve without redesigning the grid each time requirements changed.
Gave up perfect layouts in rare cases → gained lower complexity, faster iteration, and higher confidence in delivery.
Image Generator 2.0 marked a structural shift from a single model (Flux) to supporting multiple third-party providers, including Flux, Recraft, and HiDream.

This introduced significant asymmetry. Recraft alone added over 100 models with different styles, while others offered only a few—making a flat, model-first list difficult to scale without overwhelming users.
Rather than exposing models directly by provider, I organized the selection experience around visual intent.
This approach separates fast decision-making from deeper exploration, allowing users to choose based on creative intent while keeping the full catalog accessible.
Chose to organize selection around artistic style rather than exposing raw model distinctions, prioritizing user intent over technical accuracy.
Enabled fast, predictable model selection across providers with highly uneven model volumes.
(Upscale / Variation / Modify)
Editing Tool Usage
Advanced Editing Tool Usage
Ecosystem Impact
Cross-tool Conversions
More users sending images into Video, Face Swap, and Talking Photo.
Platform-level Adoption
Product Lines
The redesigned preview module was adopted across
Redesigned preview module adopted across
Engineering Impact
Reduction in layout complexity
Enabling faster implementation of future features.
What users and stakeholders said after the redesign:
-Klein, PM of the product
-Annonymous User
This project shaped how I approach system design, problem framing, and product growth. Here are the key takeaways and what I would explore next.
We expected art models to be popular; however, data shows that users preferred simple, fast defaults.
This reminded me to verify desirability early and treat usage data as a design signal, not an afterthought.
Many v1 issues were rooted in early requirements.
Good design decisions depend on good problem framing. I now prioritize upstream alignment with PM and engineering.
Good design decisions depend on good problem framing. I now prioritize upstream alignment with PM and dev team.
A/B testing model density and grouping
Personalized model recommendations
More user interviews with professional creators
Data-driven refinement of cross-tool workflows

Beyond the core redesign, I explored a set of edge cases and responsive patterns to ensure the system remains stable, predictable, and scalable across different devices, models, and real-world usage scenarios.
These explorations were not the primary focus of the case study, but they reflect how I think about product completeness, system boundaries, and long-term maintainability.
Includes processing generation, generation failure, and safety-blocked scenarios during image creation.
Covers unauthenticated users, empty generation history, and missing styles for selected models.
Shows responsive layouts across desktop, tablet, and mobile, with key controls and results preserved at every breakpoint.
Includes mobile app feature designs, an Adobe Express plugin, new product pages, and marketing covers, all designed by me.
If this work resonates, I would be glad to talk more about LLM security and applied AI systems.

Production Systems
XClaw
In applied AI environments, things move fast. I learned to test ideas quickly, work closely with PMs and engineers, and keep product decisions grounded in real user constraints.
Lanma.ai
Jul 2024 - Sep 2024

Back in 2024, before “AI agents” became everyone’s favorite buzzword, I was lucky enough to design for one. I explored new AI interaction paradigms and thought deeply about making agent intelligence usable, trustworthy, and controllable, especially in complex B2B workflows.
Mobile Now
Feb 2024 - Jun 2024

My first UX internship was at a design agency, a place full of tight deadlines, big clients, and diverse projects. I learned to design both systematically and empathetically, balancing client and user needs. Striving for pixel-perfect delivery turned me into a Figma master, giving me my first taste of design craftsmanship.
Stevens Institute of Technology
Master of Science, Computer Science
Sep 2024 - Present
Graduate School

At Stevens Institute of Technology, I strengthened my computer science foundation through systems, machine learning, and AI research. Studying in Hoboken also placed me close to New York's engineering community and helped me connect research decisions with production constraints.
Shenzhen University
Bachelor, Logistics Management
Sep 2020 - Jul 2024
Undergrad

At Shenzhen University, I built a foundation in logistics management, data analysis, and operational systems. That training taught me to trace how information, people, and resources move through complex organizations, a perspective I now apply to AI and ML systems.
I'm an applied AI engineer focused on turning research ideas into practical ML systems, trustworthy LLM workflows, and production-ready AI products.
My approach stays grounded in measurable outcomes, system ownership, and clear communication across research and product.
I believe great design is the perfect intersection of aesthetics, strategy, and engineering feasibility.
For me, design is play.
Being a AI/ML Engineer Intern feels like the perfect “impossible triangle” — it connects what I’m good at, what I love, and a career path full of growth and possibility.

My approach to design is deeply shaped by a personal philosophy that echoes Naval Ravikant’s belief in finding work that feels like play. It’s this mindset that keeps me curious, creative, and driven every day.
——Naval Ravikant
I work on applied AI at the intersection of ML research, LLM security, and systems engineering.

Across ownership verification, adversarial evaluation, and multi-agent topology inference, I treat every model as an operational system: define the threat model, measure what matters, and design for deployment.
Today, I turn research ideas into reliable AI infrastructure and products, making complex model behavior measurable, secure, and useful.

Outside of building AI systems, I keep a running archive of photos, textures, and references that sharpen how I think about interfaces, storytelling, and product taste. You can browse a few more notes and builds on my GitHub. Hiking and discovering new places help me stay grounded and inspired.
Thanks for scrolling all the way here💛 I saved three Polaroids from my own moments for you, click the shutter three times to reveal them, and feel free to drag them around.
